common_name: Mini Monstera (Piccolo Monstera, Ginny Philodendron, Rhaphidophora)

planting.note: Grow on a moss pole or bark support in bright indirect light in well-draining, chunky aroid mix; Raphidophora tetrasperma climbs vigorously and produces progressively larger, more deeply split leaves as it ascends a support — provide a tall moss pole for best results. Despite widespread common names referencing Monstera and Philodendron, it belongs to neither genus. Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Maintain moderate humidity (50%+). Propagate from stem cuttings. All parts mildly toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in well-draining, aerated potting mix on a climbing support — Mini Monstera produces its most attractive, deeply split leaves as it climbs and becomes less impressive when allowed to trail without a support. Raphidophora tetrasperma is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid native to southern Thailand and Malaysia in the Araceae family — not a Monstera, not a Philodendron, despite being widely marketed under both names. The plant produces deeply split, pinnatifid (partially divided rather than fully perforated), glossy, dark-green leaves on slender, fast-growing climbing stems. In young plants, the leaves are small and undivided; as the plant climbs, they become progressively larger (up to 12 inches) and more deeply cut into opposite pairs of oval lobes separated by incisions extending almost to the midrib — a leaf pattern strikingly similar to a miniature Monstera deliciosa. The combination of this attractive, deeply-split leaf pattern, fast growth rate, compact size relative to true Monstera, and ease of cultivation in indoor and tropical garden conditions has made Raphidophora tetrasperma one of the most popular aroids in the contemporary houseplant boom. It grows significantly faster than true Monstera and is substantially more compact, making it better suited to average indoor and small tropical garden spaces. Mildly toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Raphidophora tetrasperma species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing Aroids for tropical indoor collections
International Aroid Society — Raphidophora tetrasperma: identity and cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Raphidophora (toxic to pets)

common_name: Emerald Vine (Jade Satin Pothos, Amydrium, Green Galaxy)

planting.note: Grow on a moss pole or tree support in bright indirect light in well-draining, chunky aroid mix; Amydrium medium climbs vigorously and requires a support to develop its most attractive, large adult foliage — without a support, it trails and the leaves remain smaller. Allow the top inch to dry between waterings. Maintain moderate humidity. Propagate from stem cuttings. As the plant matures on its support, the leaves develop perforations similar to Monstera. Often sold under the incorrect trade name 'Jade Satin Pothos.' All parts toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light on a moss pole or tree support — Emerald Vine is a vigorous climber that produces distinctly larger, more attractive leaves when ascending a support compared to when trailing, and should be provided with a tall support for best ornamental effect. Amydrium medium is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid native to Southeast Asia (Thailand, Malaysia, Laos, Vietnam, Philippines, Borneo) in the Araceae family — a relatively recently recognized genus in the aroid trade, where it is commonly misidentified as a Pothos or Scindapsus species and sold under the trade name 'Jade Satin Pothos.' The plant produces climbing stems with large (8–16 inches), oval to broadly heart-shaped, leathery, deeply glossy leaves in an exceptionally rich, dark emerald-green — the glossy surface of A. medium leaves has a depth and intensity of color that has made it popular in the tropical plant collecting community. In juvenile stage, the leaves are solid, undivided, and simple; as the plant matures and climbs, the leaves develop windows (perforations) scattered across the blade surface, adding visual interest. The intense, varnished-looking green surface combined with the developing perforations on adult leaves makes Amydrium medium one of the most ornamentally appealing of the lesser-known climbing aroids. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Amydrium medium species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing Aroids for tropical indoor collections
International Aroid Society — Amydrium medium identification and cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Amydrium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Trailing Jade Peperomia (Round-Leaved Peperomia, Creeping Peperomia)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in very well-draining potting mix (adding 50% perlite to standard potting mix works well); Peperomia rotundifolia is particularly prone to root rot if overwatered — allow the top half of the soil to dry before re-watering. The trailing stems look beautiful cascading over the edge of a hanging basket. Maintain temperatures above 50°F. Mist the leaves occasionally to maintain humidity. Propagate from stem or leaf cuttings. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in well-draining, perlite-enriched potting mix — Trailing Jade Peperomia tolerates lower light than many other peperomias but produces the most compact, attractive growth in bright, indirect conditions. Peperomia rotundifolia is a small, trailing, epiphytic peperomia native to tropical South America (Peru, Ecuador, Brazil, Venezuela, Colombia) in the Piperaceae family, one of the most delicate and graceful of the trailing peperomias for hanging basket culture. The plant produces very slender, wiry, trailing or creeping stems with small (0.25–0.5 inch), round to nearly circular, thick, succulent, bright medium-green leaves spaced along the stems — the round leaf shape (the 'rotundifolia' — round-leaved) and the fine, delicate stem combined create a trailing pattern of fine-textured, emerald-green circles that looks unlike most other peperomias. The semi-succulent nature of the leaves makes it more drought-tolerant than many tropical houseplants while keeping the foliage fresh and full. In a hanging basket, the trailing stems can reach 24 inches or more, creating a fine-textured, green curtain effect. Excellent in terrariums where its delicate scale is appreciated. One of the easiest peperomias to maintain in appropriate conditions. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Peperomia species for indoor and tropical garden use
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Peperomia rotundifolia species profile
American Gesneriad and Peperomia Society — Peperomia rotundifolia cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Peperomia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Cupid Peperomia (Creeping Peperomia, False Philodendron Peperomia)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in well-draining, perlite-enriched potting mix; Peperomia scandens can be grown as a hanging basket plant, a vining plant on a small trellis, or a groundcover on a shaded patio. Allow the top inch to dry between waterings. The variegated form ('Variegata') has cream-margined leaves and is the most commonly available; the solid green form is less common. Prefers temperatures above 60°F. Propagate from stem cuttings. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in well-draining potting mix — Cupid Peperomia is tolerant of lower light conditions than many other houseplants but produces its most attractive, full growth in bright indirect conditions. Peperomia scandens is a trailing to climbing peperomia native to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America in the Piperaceae family, one of the most widely grown of the larger-leaved peperomias for its attractive trailing habit and appealing heart-shaped foliage. The plant produces long, somewhat fleshy, scandent (vining/climbing) stems with broadly heart-shaped to broadly oval, light to medium-green, slightly glossy, succulent-textured leaves 1.5–2.5 inches long. The most commonly sold form is 'Variegata,' which has the same heart-shaped leaves but with irregular, cream to ivory margins and variegation on the green background — a soft, attractive cream-and-green combination that has made this form popular as a trailing houseplant and basket specimen. The plant's trailing stems can reach 2–4 feet in length, making it effective in hanging baskets and on shelves where the vines can drape. It can also be trained up a small trellis or moss pole, where it produces an attractive, light-textured, climbing display. The common name 'False Philodendron Peperomia' reflects the Philodendron-like appearance of the heart-shaped leaves. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Peperomia species for indoor tropical use
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Peperomia scandens species profile
American Gesneriad and Peperomia Society — Peperomia scandens cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Peperomia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Cowhorn Orchid (Bee Orchid, Antler Orchid, Florida Cowhorn)

planting.note: Mount on rough-barked wood or cork or grow in an open slatted basket with coarse bark; the hollow pseudobulbs of wild plants are naturally inhabited by colonies of biting ants (the ant-orchid relationship is one of the most studied in orchid ecology), but in cultivation the plants grow and bloom without ants. Provide bright indirect to partial direct light. Water regularly in active growth; reduce in winter dry rest. The tall flower spikes (6–10 feet) require space — this is a large orchid. Listed as an endangered species in Florida. In zone 9, protect from hard freezes. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on rough bark or in an open slatted basket in bright indirect to filtered direct morning sun — Cowhorn Orchid grows most vigorously in good indirect light with air circulation and adapts to a range of conditions once established. Myrmecophila tibicinis is a large, epiphytic orchid native to southern Florida (critically endangered), the Florida Keys, the Caribbean, Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most distinctive orchids of the Florida and Gulf Coast region for several remarkable qualities. The pseudobulbs are large (6–18 inches), hollow, and shaped like cowhorns or antlers — clustered in dense masses and, in wild plants, invariably inhabited by colonies of biting ants (genus Crematogaster) that defend the plant aggressively in exchange for shelter in the hollow pseudobulbs. This ant-plant mutualism is one of the best-documented in orchid ecology. The flower display is extraordinary: tall, much-branched inflorescences 6–10 feet tall emerge from between the pseudobulbs and bear hundreds of 1-inch, bright magenta-pink flowers — the sheer height and mass of the branching spike of hot-pink flowers makes a flowering cowhorn orchid one of the most spectacular ornamental displays in Florida and Caribbean tropical gardens. Once an abundant orchid in South Florida hammocks and cypress swamps, it has been decimated by poaching and habitat loss and is now listed as endangered in Florida. A prized specimen orchid for zone 10-12. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Myrmecophila tibicinis: Florida native endangered orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Myrmecophila tibicinis (Cowhorn Orchid) conservation
American Orchid Society — Myrmecophila tibicinis cultivation and ant mutualism
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Myrmecophila (non-toxic to pets)
